exemplar.title:
How does Arundhati Roy present the impact of caste system in Kerala
on personal identities and interactions of the characters in The God of Small Things?

detailedExemplarData.title:
How does Arundhati Roy present the impact of caste system in Kerala
on personal identities and interactions of the characters in The God of Small Things?

subject.title: English A Lang & Lit
subject.description:
**IB English A Language & Literature** is a **Group 1** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **the study of language and literature through a wide variety of texts and media from different periods, styles, and cultures**, with a strong focus on **critical analysis of both literary and non-literary texts, examining how language choices create meaning and effect in different contexts**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paper 1** | Text analysis | 1h 15m | 2h 15m | 35% (SL), 25% (HL) |
| **Paper 2** | Comparative essay | 1h 45m | 1h 45m | 35% (SL), 25% (HL)  |
| **HL Essay** | Written assignment | N/A | 1200-1500 words | 25% (HL only) |
| **Individual Oral** | Oral presentation | 15 minutes | 15 minutes | 30% (SL), 20% (HL) |

strengths: The research question is clearly stated, sharply focused and effectively guides the entire investigation.
Methodology demonstrates a well-considered selection of primary and secondary sources, with explicit justification for theoretical lenses.
Topic is communicated accurately and purposefully throughout, with each section signposted back to the research question.
weaknesses: The introduction lacks an explicit outline of the methodology employed; a brief methodological overview would strengthen clarity.
The conclusion does not reflect on methodological limitations or challenges encountered.
Minor digressions into background history sometimes distract momentarily from the central focus.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work consistently fulfills all top-band descriptors for Focus and Method. The research question is clearly formulated and tightly linked to each analytical section, and the methodology combines close textual reading with a judicious range of secondary sources. Only minor omissionsâsuch as the absence of a brief methodological overview in the introduction and limited reflection on methodological limitationsâprevent a perfect, fully self-critical approach. These small gaps do not undermine the overall clarity, focus and effectiveness, so a full 6 marks is justified.

strengths: Use of subject-specific terminology (âhybridity,â âmimicry,â âmicrocosm,â âjuxtapositionâ) is accurate and consistent.
Demonstrates extensive knowledge of The God of Small Things, Keralaâs caste history and post-colonial theory, integrating primary quotations with scholarly commentary.
Secondary sources (Bhabha, Chakravarti, Spivak, Uberoi) are effectively applied to illuminate textual moments.
weaknesses: Occasional typographical errors and mischaracterisations of theorists (e.g., âSocialist Patricia Uberoiâ) detract from precision.
Reliance on tertiary sites (Wikipedia, non-scholarly blogs) weakens academic rigour in places.
Extended quotations and narrative summary sometimes disrupt the analytical focus and flow.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs command of literary concepts and contextual knowledge is strong, meriting placement in the 5â6 band. Terminology is used accurately, and sources are generally well applied. However, a handful of factual slips, typographical errors, and an over-reliance on non-scholarly sources preclude a flawless performance worthy of the full 6 marks. A conservative score of 5 reflects excellent knowledge with occasional lapses.

strengths: Research draws on a wide array of relevant literary criticism, theory and historical studies closely aligned to the research question.
Close readings are penetrating and well supported, with insightful unpacking of imagery and metaphor (e.g., âspider with bruised eyesâ).
The argument is coherent and persuasive, engaging critically with multiple viewpoints and secondary voices.
weaknesses: Some sections revert to descriptive summary rather than analytical depth, weakening consistency of critical engagement.
A few claims (e.g., uneven colonial benefits) lack sufficient empirical support or citation of scholarly data.
Over-extended historical exposition occasionally sidelines the novelâs textual analysis.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates sustained, well-developed critical thinking in the 7â9 band: research is consistently relevant, analysis is mostly well-supported, and the discussion balances perspectives in a focused argument. Minor lapsesâoccasional descriptiveness, insufficient supporting data for certain claims, and slight repetitivenessâprevent elevation into the top band (10â12). A score of 9 accurately reflects the strength and limitations of the critical engagement.

strengths: Structure meets all requirements: clear title page, accurate table of contents, logical headings and complete references.
Layout, pagination and MLA citation style are applied consistently throughout.
Visual materials (mind-map, annotated pages) are clearly labelled and integrated appropriately in appendices.
weaknesses: Occasional inconsistent use of italics and formatting of block quotations.
A few long quotations are not formatted as block quotes, affecting readability.
The conclusion feels slightly abrupt and could benefit from a brief summary linking back to the overall structure.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation fulfils the top-band criteria: the report is well structured, formatted coherently and easy to navigate. Minor inconsistencies in italics and quotation formatting, together with a somewhat abrupt conclusion, are the only areas for improvement. A full 4 marks is therefore warranted.

strandSelections.reasoning: The methodology combines close textual analysis with a judicious, if not flawless, selection of secondary literary criticism (Bhabha, Chakravarty, Spivak, Uberoi, etc.) and contextual sources on caste and colonial history.  The essay explains why each theoretical lens (e.g., hybridity, mimicry) is pertinent to the RQ, and primary evidence is systematically cited.  Minor gaps are visibleâevaluation of Wikipedia and TED-Ed sources remains cursoryâbut the overall **range, relevance and integration** of methods/sources demonstrate effective, well-considered selection characteristic of the highest band.

Font, spacing, pagination and citation style (MLA) are **consistently applied**, and visual materials (mind-map, annotated pages) are clearly labelled in the appendices.  Minor issuesâoccasional inconsistent italics, a slightly crowded mind-map image, and a few long quotations lacking full block formattingâare present but do not impede readability.  The work therefore merits the top band (3-4), with a score of 4 reflecting largely correct but not flawless application.

Research draws on a **wide array of literary criticism, historical studies and theory** that is generally current and appropriate; most sources are clearly relevant to the RQ and are integrated rather than appended.  The student demonstrates how each secondary voice (Bhabha on hybridity, Chakravarti on caste/gender, Gupta on social honour) informs the reading of specific passages.  Some sources (e.g., TED-Ed video, Wikipedia) are less scholarly and only briefly justified, which narrows claims to âexcellentâ rather than âoutstanding,â but the overall standard comfortably meets the 7-9 band.

The student states and sustains a well-defined topic from the outset, framing the investigation around the social impact of caste in Kerala as represented in Royâs novel.  The introduction succinctly situates the text, historical background and key concerns (casteism, patriarchy, post-colonial identity) and this line of inquiry is clearly revisited in each body section and in the conclusion.  Occasional digressions into plot summary or broad historical description appear, yet they do not obscure the overarching purpose; readers are consistently reminded how each point links back to the exploration of personal identities and interactions in the novel.  Overall, communication of topic is **accurate, purposeful and continuously evident**, fulfilling the top-band descriptor.

The essay develops a **coherent, persuasive argument** that threads through each thematic section and culminates in a reflective, if briefly truncated, conclusion.  Evaluation of differing viewpoints (e.g., critiques of caste portrayal, colonial benefits) demonstrates a critical stance, and the student weighs evidence to justify positions.  Minor lapsesâsuch as moments of unlinked plot summary or slight repetitivenessâintroduce small inconsistencies, yet overall discussion remains focused, balanced and critically engaged, warranting placement in the 7-9 band.

Close reading is **penetrating and usually well-supported**: imagery such as âno footprints in sandâ or Ammuâs âspider with bruised eyesâ is unpacked with attention to language, symbolism and social function, then linked to theoretical perspectives.  Counter-arguments (e.g., Spivakâs critique of exceptionalism, alternative motives for Baby Kochamma) enrich the discussion.  Where depth slips, it is due to occasional over-extended historical exposition.  Nevertheless, interpretation remains largely analytical rather than descriptive, fulfilling the upper 7-9 band.

The essay meets **all structural requirements**: title page with word count, clear table of contents, logical section headings matching the argument, complete references, bibliography, and appendices.  Transitions are smooth and the conclusion, though slightly abrupt, links back to the RQ.  The presence of minor redundancies and one or two truncated sentences indicates room for refinement, but overall structure is fully appropriate, satisfying the 3-4 band.

The student exhibits **extensive knowledge of Royâs novel, Keralaâs caste history and post-colonial theory**, weaving primary quotations with scholarly commentary.  Contextual material (e.g., Subaltern Studies, Bhabha, Chakravarti) is generally well applied to illuminate textual moments.  Nonetheless, a few factual slips (the repeated phrase âdivorced widow,â some over-generalised statements about colonial âbenefitsâ) and reliance on tertiary sites (Wikipedia) prevent a flawless record.  Hence the work is best characterised as excellent but with occasional lapses, meriting a score in the 5â6 band, closer to 5.

Subject-specific vocabularyââhybridity,â âmimicry,â âmicrocosm,â âjuxtaposition,â âmetonymy,â etc.âis used **accurately and consistently**, evidencing sound command of literary terminology.  Occasional typographical errors (e.g., âcozie,â âinvivibilityâ) and moments where critical terms are name-checked rather than fully unpacked slightly diminish precision.  However, these slips are infrequent; the overarching command of concepts supports the 5â6 descriptor, with a conservative placement at 5.

The research questionââHow does Arundhati Roy present the impact of caste system in Kerala on personal identities and interactions of the characters in The God of Small Things?ââis **clearly formulated, sharply focused and closely aligned** with Language & Literature conventions.  Throughout the essay headings (âFamily and Social Obligations,â âCasteism,â âColonialism and Post-colonial Identityâ) map directly onto the key terms of the question (âimpact,â âidentities,â âinteractionsâ), and signposting at the start and end of each section continually references the RQ.  The focus only wavers briefly when background history becomes extensive, yet analysis quickly redirects to the stated line of inquiry.  The consistent linkage between evidence, sub-arguments and the RQ warrants placement in the 5â6 band.

annotations.comment: Early in the Family and Social Obligations section, the analysis remains descriptive; strengthen the Critical Thinking by explicitly linking narrative details back to the research question.
The repeated quotation of âdivorced daughterâ could be summarised rather than quoted at length to avoid redundancy and maintain reader engagement.
The mention of Guptaâs analysis is apt but needs development; explain explicitly how his concept informs Royâs portrayal to deepen Critical Thinking.
Embedding long quotations disrupts flow; integrate key phrases into your own analysis or use shorter excerpts to improve readability.
The claim about uneven colonial benefits would be more persuasive if supported by statistical data or specific case studies from reputable sources.
The inclusion of a precise word count at the start meets the IB formatting requirement, demonstrating the studentâs attention to administrative conventions.
The research question is prominently stated and tightly focused, providing clear direction and purpose for the investigation from the outset.
The table of contents is clearly organised with accurate page references, enhancing navigability and structural coherence.

The introduction provides rich context but lacks an explicit description of the methodology; the student should briefly outline their analytical approach and selection of secondary sources to strengthen Focus and Method.
This paragraph veers into extensive plot summary rather than analytical framing; consider condensing narrative detail to maintain an academic focus on the research question.
The use of a non-scholarly blog as a source weakens the academic rigour; replace or supplement this citation with peer-reviewed literature for more reliable support.
The critique of Spivak is interesting but lacks a full citation in-text; include a proper reference to strengthen the scholarly dialogue.

Uberoi is introduced as âSocialist Patricia Uberoi,â which mischaracterises her discipline; accurately identify her as a sociologist and correct typographical errors to maintain precision in Knowledge and Understanding.
The unpacking of the âspider with bruised eyesâ metaphor is insightful, effectively linking imagery to Ammuâs oppression and enhancing analytical depth.
Aijaz Ahmed is cited as a critic but is mislabelled; ensure accurate context for each theorist to demonstrate full command of subject-specific terminology.
The discussion of Vellya Paapenâs conflict is engaging but would benefit from deeper analysis of his internal motivation, supported by additional textual evidence.
The accurate and consistent use of the term âAnglophiliaâ demonstrates a strong command of subject-specific terminology in post-colonial theory.
The application of Bhabhaâs third space to the twins is promising; extend this by drawing specific textual parallels to illustrate their âin-betweenness.â
The conclusion summarizes findings well but lacks reflection on methodological limitations; adding this would strengthen the Focus and Method criterion.
A typo âcastâ appears instead of âcasteâ here; ensure precise terminology to maintain academic credibility.
The Works Cited list is numbered rather than arranged alphabetically with a hanging indent, which diverges from standard MLA/IB conventions; revise accordingly.
Numbering entries in a bibliography is inconsistent with MLA style; IB expects an unnumbered, alphabetized list formatted with hanging indents.
The term âveshyaâ is powerful; define its cultural nuance and original language to demonstrate deeper Knowledge and Understanding.
The gender double standard is noted well; bolster this argument with historical context on patriarchal norms to enhance analytical rigor.
The fragmented quotation of âBroken the lawsâ¦â ends mid-clause; include the complete sentence to ensure full clarity in your syntactic analysis.
Relying on a general encyclopedia for colonial context limits depth; incorporate scholarly monographs or journal articles to strengthen research quality.
